<rna_modification_context>
<modification_type>m1A</modification_type>
<modification_biology>
N1-methyladenosine (m1A) occurs in tRNA, rRNA, and mRNA, deposited by TRMT6/TRMT61A. At position 58 of tRNA, it is essential for tRNA stability. In mRNA, m1A affects translation and is enriched near start codons. m1A dysregulation is linked to mitochondrial dysfunction, a hallmark of cellular aging.
</modification_biology>

</modification_biology>
</rna_modification_context>

Classify genetic variants affecting RNA modification sites by their most likely disease association. Respond with only the option letter (A, B, or C).

<rna_modification_context>
<modification_type>m7G</modification_type>
<modification_biology>

</modification_biology>
</rna_modification_context>

<modification_type>Psi</modification_type>
<modification_biology>
Pseudouridine (Ψ) is the most abundant RNA modification, formed by uridine isomerization catalyzed by pseudouridine synthases (PUS enzymes). </modification_biology>
</rna_modification_context>

<modification_type>m6A</modification_type>

N6-methyladenosine (m6A) is the most abundant internal mRNA modification in eukaryotes, deposited by the METTL3/METTL14/WTAP writer complex and removed by FTO/ALKBH5 erasers. You understand how RNA modifications (m6A, A-to-I editing, m5C, pseudouridine, ac4C, and others) regulate gene expression and contribute to aging and disease. Classify genetic variants affecting RNA modification sites by their most likely disease association. Respond with only the option letter (A, B, or C).

<rna_modification_context>
<modification_type>ac4C</modification_type>
<modification_biology>
N4-acetylcytidine (ac4C) in mRNA is catalyzed by NAT10, the only known RNA acetyltransferase. It enhances mRNA stability and translation accuracy. NAT10 is upregulated in cancer and progeria; its inhibition by Remodelin partially rescues Hutchinson-Gilford Progeria Syndrome, making ac4C directly relevant to aging.
</modification_biology>
</rna_modification_context>

<genetic_variant>
<genomic_location>chr19:10222197 (+ strand, hg19)</genomic_location>
<variant_effect>human ac4c-loss variant</variant_effect>
<confidence_level>medium</confidence_level>
<affected_gene>P2RY11 (protein_coding)</affected_gene>
<gene_region>5' UTR</gene_region>
<region_functional_context>5' UTR — affects cap-dependent translation initiation; modifications alter ribosome recruitment and translational efficiency.</region_functional_context>
<sequence_context>
  Reference (41 nt): TTTCAGCAGATGAGCTTGAACCTCAGGAGGGTTGTGGCACAATGAGGAAGGAAACGTGGGTGGAAAGGCACGCTGGCCTGCTCTGCTGGCTGGGCCAGTAACTGGGGATGGGGCTGGGGCAGGGCCCACTAAGCCACTGGTGACTGGGGGAGGGGCTGGGGAACTGGGTAGCAGACACAGGCTGAGGATCGGCACGGGAGCATGGCAGCCAACGTCTCGG
  Alternate  (41 nt): TTTCAGCAGATGAGCTTGAACCTCAGGAGGGTTGTGGCACAATGAGGAAGGAAACGTGGGTGGAAAGGCACGCTGGCCTGCTCTGCTGGCTGGGCCAGTAACTGGGGATGGGGCTGGGGCAGGGCCCACTAAGCCACTGGTGACTGGGGGAGGGGCTGGGGAACTGGGTAGCAGACACAGGCTGAGGATCGGCACGGGAGCATGGCCGCCAACGTCTCGG
  Modification site position in window: 207/41
</sequence_context>
</genetic_variant>

<question>
This genetic variant affects an RNA ac4C modification site in the 5' UTR of gene P2RY11.
Based on the modification biology, variant characteristics, and gene context, 
what is the most likely disease association of this variant?
</question>

<options>
A. Aging or age-related disease — including neurodegeneration (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's), cancer, cardiovascular disease, metabolic disorders, or other conditions strongly linked to aging biology
B. Non-aging disease — a genetic or developmental disorder without a primary aging component (e.g., congenital syndrome, rare Mendelian disorder)
C. No known disease association — this modification site variant has not been linked to a specific disease in current databases
</options>

Respond with only the option letter (A, B, or C).
C

<rna_modification_context>
<modification_type>m1A</modification_type>
<modification_biology>
N1-methyladenosine (m1A) occurs in tRNA, rRNA, and mRNA, deposited by TRMT6/TRMT61A. At position 58 of tRNA, it is essential for tRNA stability. In mRNA, m1A affects translation and is enriched near start codons. m1A dysregulation is linked to mitochondrial dysfunction, a hallmark of cellular aging.
</modification_biology>
</rna_modification_context>

You understand how RNA modifications (m6A, A-to-I editing, m5C, pseudouridine, ac4C, and others) regulate gene expression and contribute to aging and disease. Classify genetic variants affecting RNA modification sites by their most likely disease association. Respond with only the option letter (A, B, or C).

<rna_modification_context>
<modification_type>A-to-I</modification_type>
<modification_biology>
Adenosine-to-inosine (A-to-I) RNA editing is catalyzed by ADAR enzymes, recoding adenosine as inosine (read as guanosine). Critical for neural function and innate immunity. A-to-I editing efficiency declines with age and is altered in neurodegenerative diseases and cancer.
</modification_biology>
</rna_modification_context>

<genetic_variant>
<genomic_location>chr1:235612166 (+ strand, hg19)</genomic_location>
<variant_effect>human a-to-i-loss variant</variant_effect>
<confidence_level>medium</confidence_level>
<affected_gene>TBCE (protein_coding)</affected_gene>
<gene_region>3' UTR</gene_region>
<region_functional_context>3' UTR — critical region for miRNA targeting, mRNA stability signals, and AU-rich element-mediated decay; RNA modifications here directly affect mRNA half-life.</region_functional_context>
<sequence_context>
  Reference (41 nt): CAAAACAAAGGGGGTTTACAACTTGTCCTAAGTATAACAAG
  Alternate  (41 nt): CAAAACAAAGGGGGTTTACGACTTGTCCTAAGTATAACAAG
  Modification site position in window: 20/41
</sequence_context>
</genetic_variant>

<question>
This genetic variant affects an RNA A-to-I modification site in the 3' UTR of gene TBCE.
Based on the modification biology, variant characteristics, and gene context, 
what is the most likely disease association of this variant?
</question>

<options>
A. Aging or age-related disease — including neurodegeneration (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's), cancer, cardiovascular disease, metabolic disorders, or other conditions strongly linked to aging biology
B. Non-aging disease — a genetic or developmental disorder without a primary aging component (e.g., congenital syndrome, rare Mendelian disorder)
C. No known disease association — this modification site variant has not been linked to a specific disease in current databases
</options>

Respond with only the option letter (A, B, or C).
B

<modification_type>m5C</modification_type>

</modification_biology>
</rna_modification_context>

<modification_type>ac4C</modification_type>
<modification_biology>
N4-acetylcytidine (ac4C) in mRNA is catalyzed by NAT10, the only known RNA acetyltransferase. It enhances mRNA stability and translation accuracy. </modification_biology>
</rna_modification_context>

Classify genetic variants affecting RNA modification sites by their most likely disease association. Respond with only the option letter (A, B, or C).

<rna_modification_context>
<modification_type>m1A</modification_type>
<modification_biology>
N1-methyladenosine (m1A) occurs in tRNA, rRNA, and mRNA, deposited by TRMT6/TRMT61A. At position 58 of tRNA, it is essential for tRNA stability. In mRNA, m1A affects translation and is enriched near start codons. m1A dysregulation is linked to mitochondrial dysfunction, a hallmark of cellular aging.
</modification_biology>
</rna_modification_context>

<genomic_location>chr8:128750600 (+ strand, hg19)</genomic_location>
<variant_effect>human m1a-loss variant</variant_effect>

<affected_gene>MYC (protein_coding)</affected_gene>
<gene_region>Exon (coding region)</gene_region>
<region_functional_context>Exon (coding region) — a genomic region affecting nearby regulatory elements.</region_functional_context>

  Reference (41 nt): TCTACCAGCAGCAGCAGCAGAGCGAGCTGCAGCCCCCGGCG
  Alternate  (41 nt): TCTACCAGCAGCAGCAGCAGAGCGAGCTGCAGCCCCCGGTG
  Modification site position in window: 40/41
</sequence_context>
</genetic_variant>

<question>
This genetic variant affects an RNA m1A modification site in the Exon (coding region) of gene MYC.
Based on the modification biology, variant characteristics, and gene context, 
what is the most likely disease association of this variant?
</question>

<options>
A. Aging or age-related disease — including neurodegeneration (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's), cancer, cardiovascular disease, metabolic disorders, or other conditions strongly linked to aging biology
B. Non-aging disease — a genetic or developmental disorder without a primary aging component (e.g., congenital syndrome, rare Mendelian disorder)
C. No known disease association — this modification site variant has not been linked to a specific disease in current databases
</options>

Respond with only the option letter (A, B, or C).
B

</rna_modification_context>
